title: Long-Term Macrolide Antibiotic Therapy in the Treatment of Chronic Small Airway Disease Clinically Mimicking Diffuse Panbronchiolitis
abstract: Objective In the current studies, we investigated the clinical effects of long-term macrolide antibiotic therapy for patients with chronic small airway disease (CAD) that clinically and radiologically mimics but is pathologically distinct from diffuse panbronchiolitis (DPB). Patients and Methods Twenty-one Japanese patients were selected on the basis of clinical criteria for DPB and were categorized as DPB or CAD following histological evaluation of surgical lung biopsies. All patients received long-term macrolide therapy, and therapeutic results were compared for the DPB and CAD groups. Results Clinical, laboratory, radiological, and bacterial features, as well as neutrophilia in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid were strikingly similar in both groups. Longterm treatment with macrolides improved the clinical symptoms and PaO2 in both groups. There was a significant improvement in forced expiratory volume in one second (FEV1), vital capacity (VC), and %VC in patients with DPB but not in patients with CAD. Neutrophilia in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid was also reduced following therapy in DPB patients but was refractory in CAD patients. Conclusion Based on the different responses to macrolides, CAD might be associated with conditions distinct from those of DPB. Nevertheless, low-dose macrolide therapy may be applied in CAD to achieve clinical improvement, such as in respiratory symptoms and PaO2.
text: Objective In the current studies, we investigated the clinical effects of long-term macrolide antibiotic therapy for patients with chronic small airway disease (CAD) that clinically and radiologically mimics but is pathologically distinct from diffuse panbronchiolitis (DPB). Patients and Methods Twenty-one Japanese patients were selected on the basis of clinical criteria for DPB and were categorized as DPB or CAD following histological evaluation of surgical lung biopsies. All patients received long-term macrolide therapy, and therapeutic results were compared for the DPB and CAD groups. Results Clinical, laboratory, radiological, and bacterial features, as well as neutrophilia in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid were strikingly similar in both groups. Longterm treatment with macrolides improved the clinical symptoms and PaO2 in both groups. There was a significant improvement in forced expiratory volume in one second (FEV1), vital capacity (VC), and %VC in patients with DPB but not in patients with CAD. Neutrophilia in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid was also reduced following therapy in DPB patients but was refractory in CAD patients. Conclusion Based on the different responses to macrolides, CAD might be associated with conditions distinct from those of DPB. Nevertheless, low-dose macrolide therapy may be applied in CAD to achieve clinical improvement, such as in respiratory symptoms and PaO2.
main_text: Introduction Diffuse panbronchiolitis (DPB) is a distinctive form of small airway disease (1) found almost exclusively in Japan, although there are some additional reports from China and Korea (2) . Only sporadic case reports and a few short series describing DPB have been reported in Western populations (3) (4) (5) (6) . The histopathologic feature of DPB is chronic inflammation centered first on respiratory bronchioles and surrounding alveoli and then on distal membranous bronchioles with mural infiltration of lymphocytes, plasma cells, and histiocytes, and intraluminal aggregates of neutrophils. Histologically, the most distinctive feature of DPB is an accumulation of foam cells in the walls of the respiratory bronchioles, adjacent alveolar ducts, and alveoli (7) . Typical patients are middle-aged with dyspnea, have a chronic productive cough, and show obstructive or mixed ventilatory impairment on pulmonary function tests. Approximately 75% of patients have chronic sinusitis. High-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) findings are quite characteristic but not pathognomonic: the nodular shadows are distributed in a centrilobular fashion, often extending to small, branching linear areas of high attenuation, occasionally with the presence of airway dilatation and bronchial wall thickening (8, 9) . The natural history of the disease is respiratory failure leading to cor pulmonale and ultimately death. Haemophilus influenzae and Streptococcus pneumoniae are often isolated from the sputum repeatedly or continuously over a long period early in the disease course, and in most cases, these bacteria are replaced by Pseudomonas aeruginosa in advanced stages of the disease. The prognosis of this disease had been poor, with 5-and 10-year survival rates of 62.1% and 33.2%, respectively, reported in 1983. However, prolonged treat-ment with low-dose erythromycin has increased the 10-year survival rate to over 90% (10) , primarily as a result of decreased bronchiolar inflammation by a reduction in the absolute number and activity of neutrophils (11, 12) . The clinical features, including HRCT findings, have been reported to mimic those typical of DPB found either in an idiopathic form of bronchiolitis (13) or in specific clinical settings, such as rheumatoid arthritis (14-16), adult T-cell leukemia (17) , and ulcerative colitis (18) . Long-term treatment with erythromycin, however, has been less effective against these conditions than DPB (13, 16) . Therefore, the aim of this study is to evaluate the significance of long-term macrolide therapy in chronic small airway diseases (CAD) that are clinically and radiologically indistinguishable but are pathologically distinct from DPB.Patients and MethodsStudy population Twenty-one Japanese patients were recruited from Oita University and Nagasaki University Hospitals. All subjects provided written informed consent to participate in the study. Subjects enrolled in this retrospective study met the following clinical criteria for DPB established by the Japanese Ministry of Health and Welfare in 1995: 1) symptoms included chronic cough, sputum, and dyspnea on exertion (more than 6 months); 2) physical signs included coarse crackles, rhonchi, or wheezes on auscultation of the chest; 3) radiographic findings showed bilateral fine nodular shadows mainly in the lower lung fields, often with hyperinflation of the lungs [all patients underwent chest HRCT because the presence of small rounded areas of high attenuation with a centrilobular distribution, branching linear areas of high attenuation, and hypoattenuation in the peripheral lung are useful in making a differential diagnosis (8)]; 4) pulmonary function tests and blood gas values were FEV1% <70% and PaO2 <80 Torr, respectively; 5) titers of cold hemagglutinin were elevated 64-fold or higher; and 6) patients had a history of or coexistent chronic parasinusitis. Patients enrolled in this study had no history of exposure to organic or inorganic dust or drugs known to cause bronchiolar involvement, and other chronic lung diseases causing the bronchiolar involvement were excluded, except for three patients in the CAD group with human T-cell lymphotropic virus type I (HTLV-1) infection and one with rheumatoid arthritis. Patients with other obstructive pulmonary diseases, such as chronic bronchitis, bronchial asthma, chronic emphysema, and cystic fibrosis were also excluded. A disease categorization (a final diagnosis of DPB or CAD) was made by evaluating the histological findings obtained from all patients by surgical lung biopsy. The characteristic features of DPB include lymphocytes and plasma cells accumulating predominantly in the respiratory bronchioles and adjacent centrilobular re-gions, and foamy macrophages in the respiratory bronchiole walls, adjacent alveolar ducts, and alveoli with infiltration of lymphoid cells. Histologically, the most distinctive feature of DPB is an accumulation of foam cells in the walls of the respiratory bronchioles, adjacent alveolar ducts, and alveoli (7) .Therapy and clinical evaluation Because 14-membered macrolides other than erythromycin have similar clinical benefits (19, 20) , patients received 600 mg erythromycin, 150 mg roxithromycin, or 200 mg clarithromycin. These macrolides were administered daily to nine, zero, and two patients with DPB and to six, two, and two patients with CAD, respectively. Patients that developed signs or X-ray findings suggesting pneumonia or acute exacerbation of the disease prior to enrollment in the study were administered appropriate antibiotics. None of the enrolled patients had an acute pulmonary infection in the month prior to the study. Patients did not use any other medication, including bronchodilators, during the entire study, except for short-term antibiotics if their condition became acutely exacerbated. Clinical findings, laboratory studies, including microbiologic assays, functional respiratory tests, and bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) fluid findings for all of patients were assessed before and after long-term macrolide therapy, and therapeutic results were compared according to the final histological diagnosis.BAL BAL was performed in eight patients from each group before and after the therapy using flexible fiber-optic bronchoscopes (Olympus P-20 or P-240; Olympus, Tokyo, Japan) as described previously (12) . Briefly, 50 ml of saline was instilled into one of the segmental or subsegmental bronchi of the right middle lobe. The fluid was immediately retrieved by gentle suction using a sterile syringe, and the procedure was repeated three or four times. The lavage fluid was passed through two sheets of gauze and centrifuged at 500× g for 10 minutes at 4°C. After two washes with phosphatebuffered saline (PBS), cells were suspended in 10% heatinactivated fetal calf serum. The cells were then centrifuged at 500× g for 5 minutes at 4°C, the supernatant was discarded, and the cells were resuspended in PBS supplemented with 10% heat-inactivated fetal calf serum and counted with a haemocytometer. An aliquot was then diluted to a concentration of 2×10 5 cells/ml, and a 0.2 ml cell suspension was spun down onto a glass slide at 150× g for 2 minutes using a Cytospin 2 cytocentrifuge (Shandon Instruments, Sewickley, PA, USA). The slides were dried, fixed, and then stained by the May-Giemsa method. More than 200 cells were characterized using a photomicroscope.Statistical analysis Statistical analyses were performed using StatView, version 5.0 (Tokyo, Japan). A F test was used to determine whether the distributions of all continuous data were normal. When data were found to deviate from a normal distribution, they were summarized in terms of medians and interquartile ranges rather than means and standard errors (SE). Differences in the clinical characteristics between patients with DPB and CAD were compared by Student's unpaired t test or Fisher's exact probability test, and data for each parameter before and after an intervention in each group were compared using parametric (Student's paired t) or non-parametric (Wilcoxon signed-ranks) tests as appropriate. The change in pulmonary function before and after intervention between groups was compared using parametric (Student's unpaired t) or non-parametric (Mann-Whitney U) tests as appropriate. Significance was established as p<0.05.ResultsClinical characteristics of the patients Table 1 summarizes the clinical characteristics of the 21 patients, including 11 diagnosed with DPB and 10 with CAD. All of the patients underwent surgical lung biopsies before the start of therapy. Eleven patients were confirmed as having DPB. Of the 10 patients with CAD, 2 were found to have bronchiolitis obliterans (one patient was infected with HTLV-1 and one patient had associated rheumatoid arthritis), 2 had broncho-bronchioloectasis (one of whom was infected with HTLV-1) and 6 had chronic bronchiolitis (one of whom was infected with HTLV-1). In particular, inflammatory and fibrotic processes were mainly centered on membranous bronchioles, and clusters of foamy cells characteristic of DPB were not detected in CAD patients. None of these patients had any indications of lymphocyte alveolitis or interstitial pneumonia. The gender and mean age at presenta-tion were not statistically different between the two groups. The majority of patients in both groups had no history of smoking. Patients with DPB or CAD had similar chronic respiratory symptoms, including productive cough and, less frequently, dyspnea. Audible coarse crackles and chronic sinusitis were also common to both conditions. Chest CT scans demonstrated centrilobular small nodules and linear branching high attenuation in all patients, as well as bronchial wall thickening or bronchioloectasis in 72% of DPB patients and 70% of CAD patients. These are typical CT indications of DPB (8) , and the distribution of CT abnormalities was similar in both groups. The levels of FEV1%, FEV1, and PaO2 in both conditions were similarly decreased. The cold hemagglutinin titer was elevated in both groups, but there was no significant difference between the two groups. Bacteria such as Haemophilus influenzae, Streptococcus pneumoniae, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa were isolated from the sputum of 99% and 80% of DPB and CAD patients, respectively. Other infections, such as Mycoplasma pneumoniae associated with elevated cold hemagglutinin, were ruled out, and cultures for mycobacteria and fungi were negative in this study. The mean duration of respiratory illness, 7.2 years for DPB patients and 10.3 years for CAD patients, was not significantly different. BAL fluid analysis demonstrated marked neutrophilia in 73% (range=18 to 99%) of the DPB group and 80% (range=46 to 93%) of the CAD group, with no significant difference between the two groups.Effects of macrolides The duration of therapy did not differ between the groups (Table 1 sulted in the disappearance or a marked reduction in sputum production for all patients with DPB and for 8 of 10 patients with CAD. Eradication of microorganisms in sputum culture was similar in the two groups (5 of 11 DPB patients and 4 of 10 CAD patients) as demonstrated by the absence of bacterial growth in the sputum in three consecutive cultures and the cultures remained negative until the assessment point. The functional outcome of the therapy is compared in Figs. 1 and 2 . As shown in Fig. 1 , significant improvement in PaO2 following treatment was evident in both DPB (71.9±3.1 to 80.9±3.0 Torr) and CAD (68.5±3.0 to 83.3±4.5 Torr) patients. There was a significant improvement in FEV1 (Fig. 2 ), vital capacity (VC), and %VC in patients with DPB [FEV1= 1.97±0.13 to 2.78±0.17 l; VC=2.98±0.20 to 3.53±0.17 l (p<0.005); %VC=78.0±4.3 to 97.1±2.7% (p<0.005)] but not in patients with CAD (FEV1=1.59±0.16 to 1.81±0.21 l; VC= 2.41±0.19 to 2.70±0.19 l; %VC=72.1±6.1 to 81.5±6.8%). Although the serial change in PaO2 before and after treatment did not differ between DPB and CAD groups (13.9±5.4 and 23.0±7.5%, respectively), the serial changes in FEV1 and VC were greater in patients with DPB (FEV1=53.4%, range= -7.1 to 69.2%; VC=24.0%, range=-8.0% to 43.5%) than CAD (FEV1 =1.4%, range=-35.0 to 78.4%; VC=7.5%, range=-22.4% to 53.2%). However, the differences for the FEV1 and VC values were not statistically significant (p=0.18 and 0.55, respectively). Significant improvement [defined as an increase in FEV1 of more than 15% over pretreatment values based on the reversible test for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (21) ] was observed in 10 of the 11 DPB patients but in only 4 of 10 CAD patients (p<0.05 by Fisher's exact probability test). However, there was no noticeable difference between the two groups in significant improvements in PaO2 (7 of 11 DPB patients and 6 of 10 CAD patients). The functional outcome of macrolide therapy in individual patients with DPB or CAD is also shown in Table 2 . These results show that there was no relationship between the therapy period and the serial change in PaO2, FEV1, or VC. All of the study results were similar even when three patients with HTLV-1 infection and one patient with rheumatoid arthritis in the CAD group were excluded. Furthermore, similar results were obtained even if the smokers in both groups were excluded because FEV1 after macrolide treatment was significantly improved in nonsmokers but not in smokers (data not shown). The total number of cells and the percentage of neutrophils pre-and post-treatment are compared in Fig. 3 . The number of total cells before treatment did not differ between DPB [11.2×10 5 cells/ml, range=(2.3 to 299.9)×10 5 cells/ml] and CAD [8.5×10 5 cells/ml, range=(1.2 to 70.6)×10 5 cells/ ml] patients. A significant decrease in the total number of cells after treatment was observed in DPB but not in CAD patients. Additionally, the percentage of neutrophils decreased significantly following treatment in patients with DPB (before, 72.9%, range=17.6 to 98.9%; after, 10.1%, range=2.0 to 94.5%) but not in CAD (before, 79.6%, range=46.3 to 93.2%; after, 73.9%, range=1.0 to 94.2%).Discussion The present study reports 10 cases of CAD whose clinical, laboratory, radiological and bacterial features strikingly mimicked DPB, but whose pathological features differed from those of DPB. The term idiopathic could be used to describe 6 of 10 CAD patients due to the absence of any associated pathologic conditions or any reasonable etiologies for bronchiolar involvement in the past clinical history or in the present health status. Similarly, five cases of idiopathic bronchiolitis in Caucasians have recently been reported with clinical pictures and HRCT findings mimicking those of typical DPB (13) . Three of the remaining patients in our CAD group also had an HTLV-1 infection, and one case was associated with rheumatoid arthritis. Indeed, such an association has been previously reported (14) (15) (16) 22) . A nationwide histopathological study in Japan to characterize pulmonary involvement in 32 HTLV-1 carriers with symptomatic chronic pulmonary diseases demonstrated that 23 (72%) of the patients had bronchiolar rather than interstitial involvement, and 14 of these had chronic bronchiolitis (22) . Other reports have indicated that bronchiolitis occurs in association with rheumatoid arthritis (14) (15) (16) . The clinical features of rheumatoid arthritis-associated bronchiolitis are strikingly similar to those of DPB, and, although the histological profiles of both conditions are generally similar, there are some distinct features (15, 16) . Together with our findings, this indicates that, in spite of a clinical and radiological picture consistent with DPB, the histology in CAD patients is heterogeneous and different than that typically observed in DPB. Despite improvement in sputum production and PaO2 in both DPB and CAD patients, clinical improvement following long-term treatment with low-dose macrolides, especially in FEV1 and VC, was much greater in DPB patients than in CAD patients. This difference between DPB and CAD groups is probably not due to a difference in disease duration (7.2 years for DPB cases and 10.3 years for CAD) or therapy period (35.5 months for DPB cases and 23.6 months for CAD) because the clinical features and radiological findings in both groups before treatment were quite similar and because there was no relationship between the therapy period and the serial change in pulmonary function tests. Furthermore, recent reports show that clinical improvements reach a maximum within six months after the commencement of therapy, and the improvement observed after six months is stable if therapy is continued for as long as four years (23) . The population of the CAD group was heterogeneous; it included four patients with HTLV-1 infection or rheumatoid arthritis. The functional outcome, including FEV1 and VC, was similar even when these four patients were excluded from the CAD group because macrolide treatment is less effective in improving FEV1 in CAD with HTLV-1 infection or rheumatoid arthritis than in DPB patients (16, 24) . This was identical to the finding that macrolide antibiotics are of less benefit for idiopathic bronchiolitis as determined by measurement of FEV1 (13) . Additionally, the smoking history did not influence the functional outcome of macrolide treatment in either group. Macrolide treatment is now considered to be effective for DPB primarily through the inhibition of neutrophil accumu-  lation in the lung (10) (11) (12) 19) . BAL demonstrated significant neutrophilia in all participants, and prolonged treatment with macrolides significantly reduced the percentage of neutrophils in the BAL of DPB but not CAD patients independent of bacterial outcome. This indicates that neutrophil-mediated inflammation is maintained in the airway of CAD patients even after macrolide therapy and that it eventually sustains lung injury. Furthermore, the histology in CAD patients revealed that the inflammatory and fibrotic processes were mainly centered around membranous bronchioles. Although we did not find any precise reason for the difference in response to macrolide treatment between DPB and CAD patients, these results may explain the less beneficial effect towards FEV1 and VC of the CAD patients. However, clinical findings, such as sputum volume and PaO2, were improved in the CAD patients in spite of the prolongation of neutrophil-mediated inflammation. This may be because macrolides suppress the mucus and water secretion into the airway lumen (20, (25) (26) (27) . In conclusion, the findings of this study demonstrate the existence of a chronic small airway disease that is indistinguishable, both clinically and radiologically, from DPB. In addition, based on the different response to macrolides, CAD might be associated with conditions, but not a disease entity, that are distinct from DPB. Nevertheless, low-dose macrolide therapy may be applied to achieve clinical improvement in the respiratory symptoms and PaO2 of CAD patients. Furthermore, erythromycin treatment for more than 15 years in a Japanese woman with bare lymphocyte syndrome clinically mimicking DPB gradually improved the clinical manifestations including pulmonary functions and resulted in her unusually prolonged survival (28) , and this indicates that an extended macrolide treatment will be necessary for the assessment of its efficacy in some CAD patients. Additional larger scale studies are warranted to confirm the exact clinical significance of this course of treatment in CAD. However, the attending physicians should be aware of these clinical conditions.Figure 1 . 1 Figure 1. Changes in PaO2 after macrolide treatment in patients with DPB and CAD. Values are expressed as means (±SE). Dashed and dotted lines in the CAD group represent patients with HTLV-1 infection and rheumatoid arthritis, respectively.Figure 2 . 2 Figure 2. Changes in FEV1 after macrolide treatment in patients with DPB and CAD. Values are expressed as means (±SE). Dashed and dotted lines in CAD group represent patients with HTLV-1 infection and rheumatoid arthritis, respectively.Figure 3 . 3 Figure 3. Change in (A) the number of total cells and (B) the neutrophil percentage in BAL fluid before and after macrolide treatment in patients with DPB and CAD. Values are expressed as the median (horizontal bar) and range (top and bottom of the box-and-whisker plot represent the 25th-and 75th-percentile values). The whiskers outside the box represent the 10thand 90th-percentile values.Table 1 . Clinical Characteristics of 21 Japanese Patients 1 ). screening_reason: 论文讨论了大环内酯类抗生素在治疗慢性小气道疾病（CAD）中的长期效果，其中部分患者临床表现为扩散性肺浸润（DPB）。这与研究方向的抗生素治疗有效性、COPD和DPB患者的主题高度相关。
